statement.markdown:
Write a function to validate whether a given CSS selector is valid or not. A CSS selector is valid if it does not contain any pseudo-class selectors that are not supported by the browser, such as ':hover', ':focus', ':active', etc. except for ':first-child', ':last-child', ':nth-child', ':checked', and ':enabled'.

For example, the selector '.class:hover' is invalid because ':hover' is not supported by older browsers, while '.class:first-child' is valid.

You can assume that the input selector is a string and does not contain any whitespace characters.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing a CSS selector
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the CSS selector is valid or not
statement.notes: You can use the following pseudo-class selectors as supported: :first-child, :last-child, :nth-child, :checked, :enabled

examples.explanation: The selector contains the pseudo-class ':hover' which is not supported.
The selector contains the supported pseudo-class ':first-child'.
The selector contains the supported pseudo-classes ':last-child' and ':checked'.

hints.text: Split the input selector into individual pseudo-classes

editorial.title: Explanation
editorial.intuition: The key is to split the input selector into individual pseudo-classes and then check if they are supported.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach would be to check each pseudo-class individually, which is inefficient.
editorial.betterSolution: Split the input selector into individual pseudo-classes and then check if they are supported.
editorial.optimalSolution: Split the input selector into individual pseudo-classes and then check if they are supported. You can use a set to store the supported pseudo-classes for efficient lookups.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution is correct because it splits the input selector into individual pseudo-classes and then checks if they are supported.
editorial.dryRun: Dry run the solution with the example inputs to see the output.
